essence: FMCG is a volume + distribution + brand game: low-priced, frequently bought products where winning means being everywhere and top-of-mind.
Distribution reach is the deepest moat - getting product into millions of tiny outlets is extraordinarily hard to replicate.
Brand equity buys a price premium and survival against private label.
Per-unit margins are modest; profit comes from scale, mix (premiumisation), and cost discipline.
Growth = volume + price/mix; in India the levers are rural, premiumisation, and distribution expansion.

definition.oneLiner: Companies that make and sell low-cost, frequently purchased everyday products - food, beverages, household and personal care - at massive scale.
definition.jobToBeDone: Meet everyday needs reliably and affordably - the products people buy again and again without much thought.

valueChain.profitPools: Profit pools sit with strong brands (pricing power) and with whoever controls distribution reach; commoditised categories run thin and lose to private label; premium and health-positioned segments earn the best margins.
valueChain.powerShift: Value is shifting from mass-brand + general-trade dominance -> modern trade & e-commerce/quick-commerce -> D2C and data-driven premium/niche brands, as distribution digitises and consumers fragment.

primaryArchetypes: Brand / IP (brand equity as the asset)

unitEconomics.unitOfRevenue: Revenue per unit sold (across huge volumes)
unitEconomics.revenueDrivers: Volume x Price. Growth comes from volume (penetration, distribution, frequency) and price/mix (premiumisation); marketing converts to both.

costStructure.shape: Raw materials/commodities dominate COGS, plus heavy advertising & promotion (A&P) and distribution costs; margins are modest but volumes vast, so cost and mix discipline decide profit.
costStructure.leverage: Scale lowers unit costs (procurement, plants, media); strong brands let price rise faster than cost - but commodity inflation can squeeze margins fast.

moats: Distribution reach (millions of outlets - very hard to replicate)
Brand equity (decades of trust, pricing power)
competitionAndMoats.concentration: Category leaders dominate each segment (often duopolies/oligopolies), with strong regional players and a rising tail of D2C challengers.

structuralForces.regulation: Moderate-to-heavy - food safety (FSSAI in India), labelling, advertising standards, packaging/plastic rules; GST and taxation shape pricing.
structuralForces.cyclicality: Relatively defensive (everyday demand) but exposed to commodity-price cycles and rural-income/monsoon cycles in India.
structuralForces.substitution: Private label, local/unbranded products, and D2C niche brands substitute; health trends shift demand between categories.
structuralForces.supplierBuyerPower: Commodity suppliers' power swings with prices; modern-trade & quick-commerce retailers are gaining buyer power (and pushing private label); fragmented general trade keeps brand power high.

aiPenetration.maturity: Medium - strong in demand forecasting, marketing and supply chain; consumer-facing AI emerging.
keyUseCases: Consumer insight from data & social listening
aiPenetration.valueChainImpact: Biggest value in supply chain (forecasting, waste) and marketing efficiency (A&P is a huge spend); AI also speeds innovation and sharpens pricing/promo, where small % gains are large in absolute terms.
opportunities: Cut A&P waste via better targeting

ranges: Volume vs price growth: healthy is volume-led
Working capital: often low / negative for strong players
benchmarks.note: DIRECTIONAL - rules of thumb, not audited figures. Verify before quoting.

keyTerms.FMCG / CPG: Fast-Moving Consumer Goods / Consumer Packaged Goods - cheap, frequently bought everyday products.
keyTerms.Distribution reach: How many outlets stock the product (numeric) and their sales weight (weighted).
keyTerms.General trade: Traditional small retail (kirana stores); modern trade = organised supermarkets.
keyTerms.Price/mix: Revenue growth from higher prices or a richer product mix (vs volume).
keyTerms.Premiumisation: Shifting consumers to higher-priced, higher-margin products.
keyTerms.Private label: Retailer's own-brand products competing with manufacturer brands.
keyTerms.Penetration: Share of households buying the category/brand.

questionsToAskFirst: How wide is distribution reach vs competitors?
What's the premium vs mass mix, and the trend?
How exposed are margins to commodity inflation?
Modern trade / e-com / quick-commerce share and growth?
Brand health - market share & penetration trend?
rookieTraps: Confusing price-led growth (inflation) with real volume growth
Underrating distribution as the core moat
Cutting A&P for short-term margin and eroding the brand

whatsUniqueHere: FMCG is a game of tiny margins multiplied by enormous, repeated volume - won less by any single product than by being everywhere (distribution) and first to mind (brand). The two compounding moats - a network into millions of outlets and decades of brand trust - are extraordinarily hard to replicate, which is why category leaders endure. The modern threat is that digital channels and D2C brands are unbundling exactly those two moats.

indiaLandscape.marketStructure: A large, fast-growing market split between organised majors (HUL, ITC, Nestle India, Britannia, Dabur, Marico) and a vast unorganised/regional layer; general trade (kirana) still dominates but modern trade, e-commerce and quick-commerce are rising fast.
policyTailwinds: Premiumisation with a growing middle class
indiaLandscape.note: Semi-perishable - players & policy specifics refresh periodically.

similarTo: Retail - consumer demand, supply chain, thin margins

currentState.note: PERISHABLE - refresh via research before quoting. Figures approximate.
currentState.marketStructure: India FMCG: HUL and ITC the diversified leaders; rural demand recovering and premiumisation continuing; quick-commerce (Blinkit/Zepto/Instamart) rapidly reshaping route-to-market and forcing smaller-pack/assortment strategies; D2C brands proliferating in beauty and food.
